notes: DOCUMENTED TARGET — authored from the aider transport + the claudecode/codex/pi siblings; not yet validated against a live recording (aider has no provider auth in this environment, so recording is deferred until auth is available). PRIMITIVE: aider's in-chat `/run <cmd>` (documented alias `!<cmd>`) runs a shell command locally with no LLM round-trip — the direct one-to-one analog of Claude Code's `!cmd` shell-escape this scenario is modeled on (https://aider.chat/docs/usage/commands.html). The user-observable signal asserted here is the safety arc: ready→working→ready with the session NOT left stuck in working after the command exits. OBSERVES:PARTIAL CARVE-OUT (the whole reason this cell is partial, NOT yes): aider does NOT keep the shell-escape distinct from a formal tool-call/result pair. When `/run echo hello` runs, aider writes a `> Running echo hello` blockquote into .aider.chat.history.md; the parser's runningRE (^>\s*Running\s+, parser.go:69-70) routes that line through p.toolCall("Bash") (parser.go:122-124), emitting a Bash tool_result on the SAME path as `> Applied edit` (parser.go:119). This is the OPPOSITE of the claudecode reference, where the parser deliberately SKIPS the <bash-input>/<bash-stdout> wrappers so no synthetic tool call is fabricated and state stays ready throughout. CONCRETE CONSEQUENCE: the claudecode-shaped verify.tool_calls_max:0 in scenarios.json CANNOT hold for aider — exactly one Bash tool_result is emitted per /run (or !) invocation (unit-pinned by parser_test.go TestParser_ToolCall_RunningShell). The phase DSL has no tool_calls assertion form, so this spec asserts the ARC (a real working→ready cycle that does not stick) plus a final normal turn proving the session is alive; the tool_result fold is the documented partial scope, surfaced in the recipe verify/notes rather than as a phase. TRANSPORT: aider is FilesUnderCWD{.aider.chat.history.md, RawLineParser}; there is no transcript-UUID rollout session — the whole arc lives on the one proc-<PID> session row keyed by the daemon's process scanner (cf. basic-turn / agent-question-pending specs). MECHANICS: `/run echo hello` writes a `#### /run echo hello` user-prompt line (the `####` opens a turn → working), then the `> Running echo hello` Bash tool_result mid-turn, then nothing more until ~1500ms quiet → IdleFlush synthesizes turn_done (working→ready), clamped behind the SessionDetector's ~5s working-refresh poll (the assessed observes:partial latency carve-out — a delay, not a correctness gap, so the arc is still asserted). RECORDED IN INTERACTIVE MODE (drive-aider-interactive.sh: tmux REPL), NOT headless `aider --message`: headless exits at turn end and the final ready settle is lost to a process_exited race; the interactive driver keeps the REPL alive across the escape and the final turn, torn down only at script end via tmux kill-session (no Ctrl-D / /exit). SESSION-IDENTITY WORKAROUND (same as agent-question-pending and the spec/SKILL.md proc-<PID> pitfall): the live REPL may surface a transient proc-<PID> pre-session born ready at launch, so session_birth — which only asserts 'a session appears in ready within 2s of launch' — legitimately matches that pre-session; escape_start anchors to recording start with NO session pin so the matcher picks the FIRST working in the whole recording (the pre-session never goes working), and pid_bind / escape_end / final_turn_start / final_turn_end / teardown pin to escape_start's session via same_session_as so a co-occurring pre-session can never satisfy them. HOW RUNS DIFFER: the shell command's stdout, exact final-turn assistant wording, per-turn token counts, PID, session UUID, and sub-second timing all vary. Stable structurally: one ready→working→ready escape arc that does NOT stick in working, a second ready→working→ready arc for the normal prompt, a single process_exited, and a final ready state.

text: A session appears in ready within 2s of REPL launch

The /run echo hello shell-escape opens a turn → working (binds the bindable session; a transient pre-session never enters working). Aider runs the command locally and writes a `> Running` blockquote that the parser folds into a Bash tool_result — the observes:partial fold, documented in the recipe, not asserted as a distinct shell-escape here

After the shell command exits, IdleFlush settles the turn working→ready — the escape does NOT stick in working and never enters waiting. This is the load-bearing user-observable signal of the scenario
A normal user prompt drives ready→working — proves the session is still alive and a real prompt still drives the arc after the shell-escape
The final turn completes back to ready; state never entered waiting
The driver tears down the REPL (tmux kill-session) → a single process_exited on the session
